design_philosophy.core_loop: Descend from Sky Castle to defeat sin-themed demon lords across world regions, then ascend to confront final evil in underworld; repeat across escalating difficulty stages
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Tension between the Master's divine authority and Tanzra's rebellion—former servant seeks revenge; manifests as player overcoming increasingly powerful incarnations of the seven deadly sins
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Play as a winged divine being capable of flight, magic, and sword combat; restore order to a corrupted world by confronting apocalyptic evil
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Eliminates the god-game/city-building hybrid of the original in favor of pure action platforming; commits fully to combat-focused progression; heavily magic-dependent rather than sword-centric; difficulty is optional and tunable (easy mode locks out final area), signaling intentional accessibility philosophy alongside hardcore option

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Options screen difficulty selector directly controls magic charge-up time and enemy durability; easy mode restricts access to Death Heim (final endgame), granting players explicit control over progression gating and challenge scaling

progression.upgrade_system: Magic system unlocked contextually; different magic spells designed for specific situations with varying power; player discovers optimal magic applications through level design and experimentation
progression.unlock_mechanism: Each area contains two acts: first act monsters spawned by lesser demons (condition-themed), second act spawned by one of seven deadly sins (heightened challenge); final tower (Tower of Souls/Tower of Babel) and Death Heim unlock only after defeating primary six demons
replayability_hooks: Selectable difficulty modes alter mechanical pacing and enemy resilience
Magic system encourages experimentation with different spell applications across acts
Easy mode blocks Death Heim, creating distinct progression narratives by difficulty tier

world_building_techniques: Religious epic framing (Paradise Lost, Divine Comedy references—Tanzra frozen in ice lake mirrors Inferno's Satan)
Ironic stage design: demons of Doom afflict a tyrannical king in Leon who imprisons citizens for unpaid taxes
Symbolic ending: statue of Master slowly erodes as civilization becomes self-sufficient, suggesting theme of divine obsolescence

boss_design.philosophy: Seven deadly sins embodied as primary bosses (phase 2 acts), each representing specific moral failings with thematic arena design; culminates in Destruction (mechanized Pride) in Tower of Souls, then Tanzra (final form frozen in ice) in Death Heim; boss progression mirrors spiritual ascent through sin taxonomy
notable_bosses: Seven Deadly Sins demons (act 2 bosses, individually themed)
Destruction (false god/Pride, Tower of Souls final stage)
Tanzra (primary antagonist, final battle after barrier removed by Sky Castle)

faction_indicators: Master (protagonist, divine authority seeking to restore order)
Tanzra (antagonist, formerly Master's servant leading demonic rebellion)
Seven Deadly Sins (demon lords representing specific moral corruptions)
danger_signals: Tanzra's vow for revenge signals existential threat to divine order
Demon-spawning suggests systemic corruption of world regions rather than isolated threats
Death Heim emergence after initial sin defeats signals escalation to apocalyptic scale
environmental_storytelling: City of Leon depicts tyrannical governance and injustice (Doom-afflicted king imprisons citizens for taxes) as specific manifestation of sin influence
Underground prison Gratis (ironic naming) shows civilian suffering under corruption
Tower of Souls rises after primary sins defeated, signaling player progression to cosmic-scale conflict
Ending statue erosion reflects mankind's increasing self-sufficiency and divine becoming irrelevant
